name: Cedar Walton
abstract: Cedar Anthony Walton Jr. (January 17, 1934 – August 19, 2013) was an American hard bop jazz pianist.

interviews.title: Cedar Walton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

concerts.label: Jazz: The Personal Dimension: Freddie Hubbard Sextet
Ornette Coleman and His New Septet

works.t: Swingin’ the Dream: Darn that Dream

concerts.label: Kool Jazz Festival: The Blakey Legacy

works.t: Seven Lively Arts: Ev’ry Time We Say Goodbye

concerts.label: JVC Jazz Festival: Sarah Vaughan and Her Trio and Milt Jackson Quartet

quotes.text: She used to say that. Her mother said that. [chuckles] But uh, I was determined and finally, man, fast-forward, quite a while uh, uh, she was able to tell her friends that uh, she'd been to New York to hear her son play at Carnegie Hall. So that was uh, a good outcome, I think, in those who doubt it, you know, uh, uh, somebody's child's in New York. But you had to be young, I think, to uh, to, to, to go there like that. I was twenty-one, I mean. So … At age twenty two, almost to the day uh, I got drafted, which they had mandatory draft then. So I went over to Germany for a year and a half and uh, but not after, you know, not before doin' the six months of basic training at Fort Dix uh, which is near New York. And uh …
quotes.caption: Walton recalls playing Carnegie Hall at age 21
quotes.doc_title: Cedar Walton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: The first person we chose was Cedar Walton, who I had known for years and had played in one of my bands... That quintet was born, the one [that] involved Rufus Reid, Victor Lewis on drums and Cedar Walton, of course, on piano.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lanny Morgan
about_quotes.evidence: Along with myself it was Eddie Harris and Leo Wright and Don Ellis and Cedar Walton. So it was really playing with good musicians
about_quotes.doc_title: Lanny Morgan interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: tour that is called “100 Gold Fingers,” it features ten piano players — ... Cedar Walton and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: So Cedar Walton, one of my dearest friends, called and said...
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Hutcherson
about_quotes.evidence: We started doing a group called the Timeless Allstars, which was put together by Cedar [Walton] and Harold, Curtis Fuller, Billy, Buster, traveling all over Europe.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Hutcherson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Etta Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with such wonderful musicians, you know, Cedar Walton for a while. ... We used to do a lot of gigs together.
about_quotes.doc_title: Etta Jones interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 2, 1998
